commonMistakesSection.mistake: Creating indexes on low-selectivity columns like a two-value flag
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Optimizer ignores low-selectivity indexes and DML cost rises with no benefit
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Profile via ST05 and index high-selectivity columns like document number or date

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Enabling buffering on a fast-changing transactional table
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Constant invalidations defeat buffering and risk stale reads
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Buffer only stable customizing tables; leave transactional tables unbuffered

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Omitting leading key fields from WHERE clause on transactional tables
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Forces full table scan ignoring primary index
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Always include BUKRS and GJAHR (or relevant leading keys) in WHERE for BSEG/BKPF/EKKO

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Calling SELECT inside a LOOP instead of array fetch
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Causes N+1 query problem and severe performance hit
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Use SELECT INTO TABLE or FOR ALL ENTRIES to batch database access

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Using FOR ALL ENTRIES without checking empty driver table
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Empty driver returns all rows ignoring intended filter
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Always IF lt_driver IS NOT INITIAL before FOR ALL ENTRIES to avoid unintended full scan

data-dictionary-061.quickRevisionNotes:
What: Data Dictionary performance work addresses slow queries via index design, buffering, view optimization, and SQL pattern fixes.

Used for:
- Identifying and resolving slow ABAP queries on standard and Z tables
- Reducing database load via correct buffering and indexes
- Profiling ABAP-level bottlenecks via SAT/SE30

Key syntax / tables / Tcodes:
- SE11 Indexes; ST05 SQL trace; DB02 space and load; SAT runtime
- BYPASSING BUFFER for fresh reads; FOR ALL ENTRIES with non-empty driver
- Leading key fields in WHERE; CDS views in S/4HANA for analytic loads

Interview keyword: ST05 to profile, indexes on high-selectivity fields, buffer only stable tables, FOR ALL ENTRIES needs non-empty driver.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you create and manage secondary indexes in SE11?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: SE11 mein secondary index create karna: table open karo, toolbar mein Indexes tab pe jaao, Create click karo. Index name provide karo (SAP standard: table-name~3-digit-number jaise ZCUSTOM~001). Wo fields select karo jo WHERE clauses mein frequently used hote hain. Unique sirf tab mark karo jab business logic uniqueness require kare — unique index duplicate data prevent karta hai lekin constraint risk add karta hai. Index activate karo — ye database mein physically create hota hai. Management considerations: har index INSERT/UPDATE operations mein overhead add karta hai kyunki index maintain karna padta hai. Sirf un fields ke liye indexes create karo jo genuinely production queries mein frequently filtered hote hain. Un indexes ko review aur remove karo jo use nahi hote — DB02 index usage statistics dikhata hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Creating a secondary index in SE11: open the table, go to Indexes tab in the toolbar, click Create. Provide an index name (SAP standard: table-name~3-digit-number like ZCUSTOM~001). Select fields that are frequently used in WHERE clauses. Mark as unique only if business logic requires uniqueness — unique index prevents duplicate data but adds constraint risk. Activate the index — it is physically created in the database. Management considerations: each index adds overhead to INSERT/UPDATE operations as the index must be maintained. Only create indexes for fields that are genuinely frequently filtered in production queries. Review and remove indexes that are not used — DB02 shows index usage statistics.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Performance fix: Report ZREP_OPEN_ORDERS took 8 minutes on table ZCUSTOM_ORDER (3M records). ST05 showed full scan on ZSTATUS = 'OPEN'. Created index ZCUSTOM~001 on fields ZSTATUS, BUKRS (company code). After index: same report completed in 12 seconds. Index used for 80% of queries on this table. Side effect monitored: INSERT performance checked — acceptable overhead (< 5% slower). Index retained.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: Index banao aur bhool jaao check karna ki actually use ho raha hai ya nahi. DB02 → Table Analysis shows index usage count. Agar index 0 times used in months — delete it. Unused indexes = write overhead with no read benefit.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" SE11 Index Creation steps:
" SE11 → Table ZCUSTOM_ORDER → Indexes (menu)
" → Create → Index Name: ZCUSTOM~001
" → Description: Status and Date filter index
" → Fields: ZSTATUS (first), ERDAT (second)
" → Unique: No (statuses repeat)
" → Activate

" Check existing indexes:
SELECT tabname, indexname, dbindex, uniqueflag
  FROM dd12l
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_idx)
  WHERE tabname = 'ZCUSTOM_ORDER'
    AND as4local = 'A'.

" Check index field list:
SELECT indexname, position, fieldname
  FROM dd17s
  WHERE sqltab    = 'ZCUSTOM_ORDER'
    AND as4local  = 'A'
  ORDER BY indexname, position.

followupAnswerBank.question: What is the performance impact of table buffering and how do you set it correctly?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Table buffering performance: correctly buffered small static configuration tables sab reads ke liye us table ka database access near-zero tak reduce kar sakti hain — first access DB se load karta hai, subsequent accesses application server memory se served. Ek table jo per day 100,000 baar read hoti hai 1ms DB access time ke saath, full buffering per day ~100 seconds DB time bachata hai. SE11 Technical Settings mein correctly set karna: Choti tables (< few thousand records) jo rarely change hoti hain ke liye Full buffering. Key-based access patterns wali medium tables ke liye Generic buffering. Lookup tables jahan individual records accessed hote hain ke liye Single-record buffering. Frequently change hone wali tables ke liye No buffering — stale data risk performance benefit se zyada hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Table buffering performance: correctly buffered small static configuration tables can reduce database access for that table to near-zero for all reads — first access loads from DB, subsequent accesses served from application server memory. For a table read 100,000 times per day with 1ms DB access time, full buffering saves ~100 seconds of DB time per day. Setting correctly in SE11 Technical Settings: Full buffering for small tables (< few thousand records) that change rarely. Generic buffering for medium tables with key-based access patterns. Single-record buffering for lookup tables where individual records are accessed. No buffering for tables that change frequently — stale data risk outweighs performance benefit.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Buffering optimization: Customizing table ZCFG_PLANT_CTRL had 500 rows, was read at every sales order save (hundreds per hour), rarely changed (weekly). No buffering set. SM56 showed zero buffer hits. Added full buffering. Next week: SM56 showed 99.2% buffer hit rate. DB load for that table reduced from 300 reads/minute to 3 reads/minute (only on buffer miss/refresh). No stale data issues as data only changes weekly (acceptable buffer timeout).
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: 'Performance ke liye' sab tables ko buffer karna bina frequency analysis kiye. Buffer only config/static tables. Business transactional tables (VBAK, MARA, etc.) — never buffer. Agar table din mein 10+ baar change hoti hai — buffer nahi karo.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" SE11 Technical Settings: Buffering options
" Buffering Not Allowed: PUFFERUNG = ' '
" Buffering Allowed but Switched Off: PUFFERUNG = 'P' (old)
" Fully Buffered: PUFFERUNG = 'X'
" Partially (Generic): PUFFERUNG = 'G', SHLPNAME = generic key
" Single-Record: PUFFERUNG = 'S'

" Check buffer hit rate (SM56 programmatic):
SELECT *
  FROM snap_stattab  " buffer statistics
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_stats)
  WHERE tabname = 'ZCFG_PLANT_CTRL'.

" Force bypass for testing stale data:
SELECT SINGLE * FROM zcfg_plant_ctrl
  BYPASSING BUFFER
  INTO @DATA(ls_cfg) WHERE zplant = @lv_plant.

followupAnswerBank.question: How does a poorly designed view cause performance problems?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: View performance problems in se aate hain: bina proper filter pushdown ke large-table JOINs, non-selective WHERE conditions jo full join evaluation force karte hain, aur multiple table JOINs jahan join sequence database dwara optimized nahi hota. Ek classic SE11 database view VBAK + VBAP + KNA1 + MARA join karte hue — agar bina selective filters ke query hota hai, database ko multiple tables se millions of records join karne padte hain. Detection: ST05 view query ko long duration aur large row estimates ke saath dikhata hai. Fix: SE11 database views mein, ensure karo ki JOIN fields indexed hain. S/4HANA mein, associations use karte hue CDS views se replace karo — associations lazy hote hain: sirf tab evaluated jab associated entity ke fields query mein explicitly access kiye jaate hain.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: View performance problems arise from: large-table JOINs without proper filter pushdown, non-selective WHERE conditions that force full join evaluation, and multiple table JOINs where the join sequence is not optimized by the database. A classic SE11 database view joining VBAK + VBAP + KNA1 + MARA — if queried without selective filters, the database must join millions of records from multiple tables. Detection: ST05 shows the view query with long duration and large row estimates. Fix: in SE11 database views, ensure JOIN fields are indexed. In S/4HANA, replace with CDS views using associations — associations are lazy: only evaluated when the associated entity's fields are explicitly accessed in the query.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Performance issue: SE11 database view ZCUSTOM_ORDER_VIEW joining 5 tables. Report using this view without date filter ran for 20 minutes. ST12 trace: 95% time in database JOIN operation. Fix: redesigned as CDS view with associations instead of static JOINs — associations only evaluated when fields accessed. Added mandatory date filter in report. Execution time dropped to 45 seconds. Further optimization: pushed filters to WHERE on base table before JOIN.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: SE11 view mein sab possible columns SELECT * karna. View pe SELECT * — jo columns join karta hai unko bhi read karta hai database — unnecessary work. Always project only required columns. In CDS views: use projection to list only needed fields.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Problematic: SE11 view with 4-table JOIN
" View ZCUSTOM_ORDER_VIEW:
" VBAK JOIN VBAP JOIN KNA1 JOIN MARA
" No selective filter possible at view level

" Better: CDS view with associations (lazy)
@AbapCatalog.sqlViewName: 'ZCDS_ORD_V'
define view ZCDS_OrderView as
  select from vbak as o
    association [0..1] to kna1 as _cust
      on o.kunnr = _cust.kunnr
  { key o.vbeln, o.kunnr, o.erdat,
    _cust.name1  " Join to KNA1 only when name1 accessed
  } where o.erdat >= '20240101'

followupAnswerBank.question: What is a wide table problem and how does it affect performance?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Wide table problem: 100+ columns wali ek table jahan programs typically sirf 10-20 chahiye. Performance impact network transfer mein hai — even agar database rows efficiently retrieve karta hai, per row sab 100 columns ko application server tak network par transmit karna wasteful hai. Million-row result set 100 columns 500 bytes each ke saath, ye 50GB data transfer hai 10 columns ke 5GB ke versus. DDIC design principle: tables narrow aur cohesive rakho — har table ek entity ko clearly represent kare. Data normalize karo, ek table mein har possible attribute add karne ke bajaye. Existing wide tables ke saath kaam karte time: hamesha SELECT mein explicitly required columns name karo — production ABAP code mein SELECT star kabhi nahi.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Wide table problem: a table with 100+ columns where programs typically need only 10-20. The performance impact is in network transfer — even if the database retrieves rows efficiently, transmitting all 100 columns per row over the network to the application server is wasteful. For a million-row result set with 100 columns at 500 bytes each, that is 50GB of data transfer versus 5GB for 10 columns. DDIC design principle: keep tables narrow and cohesive — each table should represent one entity clearly. Normalize data rather than adding every possible attribute to one table. When working with existing wide tables: always explicitly name required columns in SELECT — never SELECT star in production ABAP code.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Design review finding: Custom entity table ZCUSTOM_ENTITY had 180 columns — vendor info, customer info, material info, financial info all combined. No one query needed more than 20 columns. Proposed split: 4 narrower tables (45 columns each) by entity type. After split: average SELECT data transfer reduced 75%. Memory footprint in internal tables reduced. Buffer efficiency improved as rows smaller. Migration of existing data took 2 days but long-term performance gain justified it.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: New fields ko 'convenience ke liye' existing table mein add karte rehna without normalizing. Every new field on a table makes it wider. Code reviews should flag SELECT * on wide tables. DDIC design reviews should question adding unrelated fields to existing tables.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Wide table problem - always select specific fields:
" BAD: SELECT * on wide table with 150 columns
SELECT * FROM zcustom_entity
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_all).  " 150 columns transferred!

" GOOD: Select only needed columns
SELECT zent_id, zent_name, zent_type, zcreated_by
  FROM zcustom_entity
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_needed)  " Only 4 columns
  WHERE zent_type = 'VENDOR'.

" Check column count of custom tables:
SELECT tabname, COUNT( * ) AS col_count
  FROM dd03l
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_wide)
  WHERE tabname LIKE 'Z%' AND as4local = 'A'
  GROUP BY tabname
  HAVING COUNT( * ) > 50
  ORDER BY col_count DESCENDING.

followupAnswerBank.question: How does missing leading key field in WHERE clause cause performance problems?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Database mein primary key index SE11 mein defined key field sequence se ordered hota hai. Primary index ko efficiently use karne ke liye, WHERE clause mein key ke leftmost positions se fields included hone chahiye. Example: table key MANDT + BUKRS + GJAHR + BELNR + BUZEI hai. Sirf BELNR ke saath WHERE clause primary key index use nahi kar sakta — ise sab records scan karne padenge ya secondary index use karna padega. BUKRS + GJAHR + BELNR ke saath WHERE clause position 2 se aage index use karta hai, lekin pehle MANDT filter karna padta hai (SAP automatically karta hai). WHERE clauses mein hamesha leading key fields include karo. Agar business requirement sirf trailing key fields pe filtering force karti hai — us field se start hone wala ek secondary index create karo.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Primary key index in a database is ordered by the key field sequence defined in SE11. To use the primary index efficiently, WHERE clause must include fields from the left-most positions of the key. Example: table key is MANDT + BUKRS + GJAHR + BELNR + BUZEI. A WHERE clause with only BELNR cannot use the primary key index — it must scan all records or use a secondary index. A WHERE clause with BUKRS + GJAHR + BELNR uses the index from position 2 onward, but must filter MANDT first (SAP does this automatically). Always include leading key fields in WHERE clauses. If business requirement forces filtering on trailing key fields only — create a secondary index starting with that field.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Performance bug: Year-end report filtered ZCUSTOM_JOURNAL only on ZJOURNAL_TYPE (5th key field, key order: MANDT, BUKRS, GJAHR, ZZPERIOD, ZJOURNAL_TYPE). No leading fields in WHERE. Full table scan on 8M records. Report took 45 minutes. Fix: redesigned WHERE to include BUKRS and GJAHR (program already knew them). Index used correctly. Report time: 90 seconds.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: WHERE clause mein interesting condition (ZSTATUS = 'OPEN') pehle likhna, leading key fields baad mein ya bilkul nahi. ABAP Open SQL aur underlying DB optimizer consider karte hain kaunsa index available hai — leading key coverage is what determines index use.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Table key order: MANDT, BUKRS, GJAHR, BELNR, BUZEI

" BAD: Missing leading fields BUKRS and GJAHR
SELECT belnr, buzei, hkont, hsl
  FROM zcustom_journal
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_bad)
  WHERE belnr = @lv_belnr.  " No BUKRS, GJAHR - full scan!

" GOOD: Include leading key fields
SELECT belnr, buzei, hkont, hsl
  FROM zcustom_journal
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_good)
  WHERE bukrs = @lv_bukrs
    AND gjahr = @lv_gjahr
    AND belnr = @lv_belnr.  " All leading fields included

" Verify key field order:
SELECT fieldname, position, keyflag
  FROM dd03l WHERE tabname = 'ZCUSTOM_JOURNAL' AND as4local = 'A'
  ORDER BY position.

followupAnswerBank.question: How does lock contention relate to Data Dictionary design and performance?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: SE11 mein defined lock objects locking ki granularity determine karte hain. Sirf table name aur koi specific lock fields wala lock object table level pe lock karega — ek process sab kuch lock karta hai. Specific key fields (e.g., order number) wala lock object row-level locking enable karta hai — sirf wo specific record locked hota hai. Poor lock object design — wrong granularity pe locking — high-concurrency scenarios mein lock contention cause karta hai: order processing 50 concurrent users ke saath, sab ek doosre ke locks ke liye wait karte hue. Design principle: lock objects ko sab se specific key fields ke saath define karo possible. Lock modes correctly use karo: writing ke time sirf E (exclusive), read-protect ke liye S (shared), specific scenarios ke liye X (exclusive non-cumulative).
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Lock objects defined in SE11 determine the granularity of locking. A lock object with only the table name and no specific lock fields will lock at the table level — one process locks everything. A lock object with specific key fields (e.g., order number) enables row-level locking — only that specific record is locked. Poor lock object design — locking at wrong granularity — causes lock contention in high-concurrency scenarios: order processing with 50 concurrent users, all waiting for each other's locks. Design principle: define lock objects with the most specific key fields possible. Use lock modes correctly: E (exclusive) only when writing, S (shared) for read-protect, X (exclusive non-cumulative) for specific scenarios.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Throughput problem: Order processing program had lock object EZCUSTOM_ORDER locking on BUKRS (company code) only — entire company's orders locked per user. 20 concurrent users = 20 users waiting in queue. Redesigned lock object: added VBELN (sales order number) as lock field — now only the specific order is locked. Throughput improved from 20 orders/minute (serialized) to 200+ orders/minute (parallel).
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: Lock object mein sirf mandatory fields dena without thinking about lock granularity. More specific key fields = finer lock granularity = better concurrency. Always think: 'What is the smallest unit of data I need to exclusively lock?' That determines lock object field list.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" SE11 Lock Object: EZCUSTOM_ORDER
" Lock Table: ZCUSTOM_ORDER
" Lock mode: E (Exclusive)

" BAD design: Lock on BUKRS only - table-level contention
" Lock Object fields: MANDT, BUKRS
" Result: one user locks ALL orders for a company code

" GOOD design: Lock on specific order
" Lock Object fields: MANDT, BUKRS, VBELN
" Result: one user locks only their specific order

" Usage in ABAP:
CALL FUNCTION 'ENQUEUE_EZCUSTOM_ORDER'
  EXPORTING mode_zcustom_order = 'E'
             mandt              = sy-mandt
             bukrs              = lv_bukrs
             vbeln              = lv_vbeln
  EXCEPTIONS foreign_lock       = 1.

followupAnswerBank.question: What is the performance impact of using SELECT FOR ALL ENTRIES with Data Dictionary objects?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: SELECT FOR ALL ENTRIES database aur entries ki number ke hisaab se database-level IN clauses ya multiple queries mein translate hota hai. Performance risks: agar driving internal table empty hai, SAP entire target table read karta hai — large tables ke liye catastrophic. Agar driving table mein too many entries (typically > 10,000) hain, IN clause too large ho jaata hai, database errors ya row-by-row reads pe fall back cause karta hai. DDIC design impact: target table mein FOR ALL ENTRIES join condition mein use hone wale fields pe indexes hone chahiye. Un fields pe index ke bina, har entry lookup ek full scan cause karta hai. Best practice: SELECT FOR ALL ENTRIES se pehle hamesha driving table non-empty check karo, aur ensure karo ki target table mein join fields pe appropriate indexes hain.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: SELECT FOR ALL ENTRIES translates to database-level IN clauses or multiple queries depending on the database and number of entries. Performance risks: if the driving internal table is empty, SAP reads the entire target table — catastrophic for large tables. If the driving table has too many entries (typically > 10,000), the IN clause becomes too large, causing database errors or falling back to row-by-row reads. DDIC design impact: the target table should have indexes on the fields used in the FOR ALL ENTRIES join condition. Without an index on those fields, each entry lookup causes a full scan. Best practice: always check driving table not empty before SELECT FOR ALL ENTRIES, and ensure the target table has appropriate indexes on the join fields.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Performance bug: Report used SELECT FOR ALL ENTRIES on ZCUSTOM_ORDER with driving table lt_vbak. In production, lt_vbak had 150,000 entries. Database query timed out — IN clause with 150K values not supported. Fix: added pagination — process lt_vbak in chunks of 5,000 entries. Also added secondary index on ZCUSTOM_ORDER on the join fields. Execution time went from timeout to 8 minutes (acceptable for batch).
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: SELECT FOR ALL ENTRIES use karna bina empty check ke. IF lt_source IS INITIAL. RETURN/skip the SELECT. ENDIF. — this single check prevents full table scan disasters. Most critical check in any FOR ALL ENTRIES usage.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" CORRECT: SELECT FOR ALL ENTRIES with safety checks
IF lt_sales_orders IS INITIAL.
  RETURN.  " Safety check - avoid full table read
ENDIF.

" Ensure driving table not too large:
IF lines( lt_sales_orders ) > 50000.
  " Process in chunks
ENDIF.

SELECT vbeln, zstatus, zcreated_by
  FROM zcustom_order
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_custom)
  FOR ALL ENTRIES IN @lt_sales_orders
  WHERE vbeln = @lt_sales_orders-vbeln.
" Ensure ZCUSTOM_ORDER has index on VBELN field
" for this to perform well

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you use DB02 to analyze Data Dictionary performance issues?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: DB02 SAP tables aur indexes ke liye database-level analysis provide karta hai. DDIC performance investigation ke liye: DB02 pe navigate karo, table name enter karo, table size dekho (row count, storage size), existing indexes, aur optionally index usage statistics. Slow query reports wali large tables (millions of rows, GB storage) ko index analysis chahiye. DB02 dikha sakta hai kaunse indexes exist karte hain aur kya wo use ho rahe hain. DB02 ke saath integrated kuch database platforms query optimizer analysis ke based pe missing index recommendations provide karte hain. DB02 table fragmentation bhi dikhata hai — overly fragmented tables reorganization se benefit le sakti hain. Complete performance diagnosis ke liye DB02 ko ST05 SQL trace ke saath use karo: ST05 dikhata hai kaunsi query slow hai, DB02 table size aur index availability dikhata hai explain karne ke liye ki kyun.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: DB02 provides database-level analysis for SAP tables and indexes. For DDIC performance investigation: navigate to DB02, enter table name, view table size (row count, storage size), existing indexes, and optionally index usage statistics. Large tables (millions of rows, GB of storage) with slow query reports need index analysis. DB02 can show which indexes exist and whether they are used. Some database platforms integrated with DB02 provide missing index recommendations based on query optimizer analysis. DB02 also shows table fragmentation — overly fragmented tables may benefit from reorganization. Use DB02 alongside ST05 SQL trace for complete performance diagnosis: ST05 shows which query is slow, DB02 shows table size and index availability to explain why.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: DB02 analysis: ZCUSTOM_TXN table reported slow queries. DB02: table size 4.2GB, 8.5M rows, 2 indexes only (primary + one secondary). ST05 showed slow query filtering on ZCREATED_DATE field — no index on that field. DB02 confirmed no date-based index. Created SE11 secondary index on ZCREATED_DATE + ZSTATUS. DB02 analysis after one week: new index showed 45,000 uses — highly utilized. Query response time improved from 12 seconds to 0.4 seconds.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: Index create karna bina DB02 current state check kiye. Maybe index already exists under different name. Or table is too small (< 100K rows) to benefit from index. DB02 first — understand current state — then decide.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" DB02 programmatic equivalent - check table stats:
" DB02 uses database-specific queries
" ABAP approximation using DBSTATC/DBSTTORA:
SELECT tabname, rowcount, sizeinfo
  FROM dbstatc
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_stats)
  WHERE tabname = 'ZCUSTOM_TXN'.

" Check indexes from SE11 metadata:
SELECT a~tabname, a~indexname, a~dbindex,
       b~fieldname, b~position
  FROM dd12l AS a JOIN dd17s AS b
    ON a~sqltab = b~sqltab AND a~indexname = b~indexname
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_idx_detail)
  WHERE a~tabname   = 'ZCUSTOM_TXN'
    AND a~as4local  = 'A'
  ORDER BY a~indexname, b~position.

data-dictionary-062.easyUnderstanding:
Simple words mein samjho:

Authorization issue Data Dictionary mein matlab koi user wo kaam nahi kar sakta jo use karna chahiye — ya galat user kuch kar sakta hai jo use nahi karna chahiye.

Sochho ek office building ka scenario. Har employee ka ID card hai. Doors pe sensors hain. HR room mein sirf HR employees jaate hain — unka ID card specifically HR room access deta hai. Accounts room mein sirf Finance team. IT room mein IT team. Agar kisi ka ID card galat configured hai — wo kisi room mein nahi jaata (frustrating) ya galat room mein chala jaata hai (security risk).

SAP Data Dictionary mein yahi hota hai:
- Table pe ABAP authorization object lagta hai — S_TABU_DIS activity
- SM30 maintenance mein Authorization Group set hota hai Table Maintenance Generator mein
- SE11 se sirf authorized users tables create ya modify kar sakte hain — S_DEVELOP authorization
- Custom tables mein apna authorization object define kar sakte ho SE11 mein (lock object ke saath field-level auth)

Authorization issue symptoms: 'No authorization' message, SM30 mein table nahi milti, SE11 changes allow nahi ho rahi.

Fix karne ke liye: SU53 transaction se dekho kaunsa authorization check fail hua, phir role mein add karo.
data-dictionary-062.hinglishMasterExplanation:
Data Dictionary authorization issues — comprehensive understanding:

**Auth Layer 1: SE11 Development Authorization**
- Object: S_DEVELOP
- Activities: 01=Create, 02=Change, 03=Display, 06=Delete, 07=Activate
- Field OBJTYPE: 'TABD' (table), 'DOMA' (domain), 'DTEL' (data element), 'SRCH' (search help)
- Without S_DEVELOP: developer cannot open SE11 to create/change DDIC objects
- Typical issue: junior developer cannot activate a table — missing OBJTYPE 'TABD' with activity 07

**Auth Layer 2: Table Content Access — S_TABU_DIS**
- Controls who can VIEW or CHANGE data in tables via SE16/SE16N/SM30
- DICBERCLS = Authorization Group (defined in table TDDAT)
- ACTVT: 02=Change, 03=Display
- Without S_TABU_DIS 03: user cannot view table in SE16
- Without S_TABU_DIS 02: user cannot change data via SM30

**Auth Layer 3: Table Maintenance — Authorization Group in TMG**
- When creating Table Maintenance Generator: Authorization Group field
- &NC& = No authorization check (wide open — development only!)
- Custom auth group: ZCFG = only users with ZCFG group can access SM30
- TDDAT table stores: table name → authorization group mapping
- If auth group set but no role has it: all users get 'No authorization' on SM30

**Auth Layer 4: Program-Level Authorization on DDIC Fields**
- Custom programs can implement field-level authorization using AUTHORITY-CHECK
- Auth object linked via data element annotation (rarely used directly)
- More common: custom program checks authorization before displaying/changing specific fields

**Auth Layer 5: Client-Dependent vs Client-Independent Table Access**
- S_TABU_CLI: authorization for client-independent table access
- Changing client-independent tables requires this special authorization
- Very sensitive — only basis/admin teams should have S_TABU_CLI with change

**Auth Layer 6: Transport Authorization**
- S_CTS_ADMI: control over transport requests
- DDIC changes in transport requests need S_TRANSPRT for release
- Without this: developer cannot release transport with DDIC objects

**Diagnosis Tools:**
- SU53: Last failed authorization check for a user — shows exact object, field, value
- SUIM: User Information System — who has which authorization
- ST01: Authorization trace — trace all auth checks for a user action
- SU21: Authorization object definitions
- SM30: Table TDDAT — shows authorization group assignments
data-dictionary-062.interviewMeKyaBolnaHai:
Interview Me Bolna Hai:
'Data Dictionary authorization issues appear in two main areas. First, developer authorization via S_DEVELOP — this controls who can create, change, activate, or delete DDIC objects in SE11. If a developer reports they cannot activate a table, SU53 will show S_DEVELOP with OBJTYPE TABD and activity 07 (activate) as the failing check.

Second, table content authorization via S_TABU_DIS and authorization groups — this controls who can view or maintain table data via SE16 or SM30. The Table Maintenance Generator has an Authorization Group field. If set incorrectly, users get No Authorization on SM30. The fix is either adding the authorization group to the user's role in PFCG, or changing the authorization group in the TMG if it is wrong.

A third important area is S_TABU_CLI for client-independent table access — only basis administrators should have change access to client-independent tables. If a business user accidentally has this authorization, it is a security risk.

My diagnosis approach: always start with SU53 in the affected user's context immediately after they see the authorization error — SU53 shows the exact authorization object, field name, and value that failed. This gives a precise fix target rather than guessing.

Hinglish tip: Authorization mein SU53 ka naam zaroor lo — ye tool interviewer ko pata chalta hai ki aapko diagnosis process pata hai, sirf theory nahi. Bolo: SU53 first, phir PFCG mein fix, phir basis ko transport karo role.'
data-dictionary-062.codeExamples:
* Authorization investigation for Data Dictionary issues

" Check authorization group for a table (TDDAT):
SELECT tabname, cclass
  FROM tddat
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_auth_grp)
  WHERE tabname = 'ZCFG_CONFIG'.
" cclass = authorization group (e.g. 'ZCFG', '&NC&')

" ABAP: Check if current user has S_TABU_DIS authorization
AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'S_TABU_DIS'
  ID 'DICBERCLS' FIELD 'ZCFG'
  ID 'ACTVT'     FIELD '03'.  " 03 = Display
IF sy-subrc <> 0.
  MESSAGE 'No authorization to display config table' TYPE 'E'.
ENDIF.

" ABAP: Check S_DEVELOP for table activation
AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'S_DEVELOP'
  ID 'DEVCLASS' FIELD '*'
  ID 'OBJTYPE'  FIELD 'TABD'
  ID 'OBJNAME'  FIELD 'ZCUSTOM_TABLE'
  ID 'P_GROUP'  FIELD '*'
  ID 'ACTVT'    FIELD '07'.  " 07 = Activate
IF sy-subrc <> 0.
  WRITE: / 'User cannot activate tables in SE11'.
ENDIF.
data-dictionary-062.interviewScriptHinglish: Data Dictionary authorization issues typically S_TABU_DIS (authorization group ke through table maintenance), S_TABU_CLI (cross-client tables), aur S_DEVELOP (DDIC object development) involve karte hain. SM30 access ke liye S_TABU_DIS correct authorization group ke saath (TDDAT mein set hota hai) aur activity values jaise 02 change ya 03 display chahiye. S_TABU_CLI critical hai aur production mein tightly restricted hota hai. SU53 use karke user ke liye last failed authorization check trace karo, phir PFCG ke through role update karo. Field-level access SM30 events ya BAPIs mein custom AUTHORITY-CHECK logic ke through control ho sakta hai. Transport authorization (S_TRANSPRT) bhi DDIC object transports ke liye relevant hota hai.
data-dictionary-062.interviewScriptEnglish: Data Dictionary authorization issues typically involve S_TABU_DIS (table maintenance by authorization group), S_TABU_CLI (cross-client tables), and S_DEVELOP (DDIC object development). SM30 access requires S_TABU_DIS with the correct authorization group (set in TDDAT) and activity values such as 02 change or 03 display. S_TABU_CLI is critical and tightly restricted in production. Use SU53 to trace the last failed authorization check for a user, then update the role via PFCG. Field-level access can be controlled via SM30 events or custom AUTHORITY-CHECK logic in BAPIs. Transport authorization (S_TRANSPRT) is also relevant for DDIC object transports.

data-dictionary-062.quickRevisionNotes:
What: DDIC authorization controls who can view, change, and maintain tables via objects like S_TABU_DIS, S_TABU_CLI, and S_DEVELOP.

Used for:
- Restricting SM30 maintenance access by authorization group
- Controlling cross-client maintenance via S_TABU_CLI
- Limiting developer access via S_DEVELOP by package/object

Key syntax / tables / Tcodes:
- S_TABU_DIS DICBERCLS+ACTVT; S_TABU_CLI; S_DEVELOP
- TDDAT table for auth group; PFCG roles; SU53/ST01 traces
- Transport auth S_TRANSPRT

Interview keyword: SU53 to diagnose; assign auth group in TMG; restrict S_TABU_CLI in production.

followupAnswerBank.question: What is S_TABU_DIS and how does it control table access?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: S_TABU_DIS SAP authorization object hai jo SE16, SE16N, aur SM30 ke through table contents tak access control karta hai. Iske do key fields hain: DICBERCLS (table authorization group, TDDAT table mein stored) aur ACTVT (activity: 02=Change, 03=Display). Jab user SE16 mein table display karne ki koshish karta hai, SAP table ke authorization group aur ACTVT 03 ke saath S_TABU_DIS check karta hai. SM30 maintenance ke liye, ACTVT 02 check hota hai. Fix karne ke liye: TDDAT se table ka authorization group determine karo, phir PFCG mein user ke role mein us group aur appropriate activity ke saath S_TABU_DIS add karo.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: S_TABU_DIS is the SAP authorization object controlling access to table contents via SE16, SE16N, and SM30. It has two key fields: DICBERCLS (the table authorization group, stored in TDDAT table) and ACTVT (activity: 02=Change, 03=Display). When a user tries to display a table in SE16, SAP checks S_TABU_DIS with the table's authorization group and ACTVT 03. For SM30 maintenance, ACTVT 02 is checked. To fix: determine the table's authorization group from TDDAT, then add S_TABU_DIS with that group and appropriate activity to the user's role in PFCG.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Authorization ticket: Business analyst needed to view custom config table ZCFG_ROUTES in SE16 for audit. They got 'No authorization for table display'. SU53 showed: S_TABU_DIS, DICBERCLS = 'ZCFG', ACTVT = '03' failed. Checked TDDAT: ZCFG_ROUTES has auth group 'ZCFG'. Basis added S_TABU_DIS with DICBERCLS = 'ZCFG' and ACTVT = '03' to user's display role. Issue resolved — user could view table.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: S_TABU_DIS mein DICBERCLS = '*' (all groups) dena for all users. This gives access to all tables including sensitive finance and HR tables. Always specify exact authorization group per role — minimum necessary access principle.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Check table authorization group:
SELECT tabname, cclass FROM tddat
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_ag)
  WHERE tabname IN ('ZCFG_ROUTES', 'ZCFG_PLANT').

" PFCG role: add S_TABU_DIS
" Object: S_TABU_DIS
" DICBERCLS: ZCFG (exact group, not *)
" ACTVT: 03 (display only) or 02+03 (change+display)

" Verify authorization check in ABAP:
AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'S_TABU_DIS'
  ID 'DICBERCLS' FIELD 'ZCFG'
  ID 'ACTVT'     FIELD '03'.
WRITE: / COND #( WHEN sy-subrc = 0 THEN 'Authorized'
                 ELSE 'Not authorized' ).

followupAnswerBank.question: How does S_DEVELOP authorization work for DDIC developers?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: S_DEVELOP control karta hai kaun ABAP development objects including sab SE11 DDIC objects create, modify, activate, aur delete kar sakta hai. Key fields: OBJTYPE object category specify karta hai — tables ke liye TABD, domains ke liye DOMA, data elements ke liye DTEL, search helps ke liye SHLP, lock objects ke liye ENQD. ACTVT: 01=Create, 02=Change, 03=Display, 06=Delete, 07=Activate. Developer ko full DDIC development karne ke liye activities 01, 02, 07 chahiye. Sirf activity 03 read-only SE11 access deta hai. Finer control ke liye DEVCLASS aur OBJNAME specific packages ya object names tak restrict kar sakte hain. Basis team S_DEVELOP assignments manage karta hai — typically DEVELOPER role template ke through.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: S_DEVELOP controls who can create, modify, activate, and delete ABAP development objects including all SE11 DDIC objects. Key fields: OBJTYPE specifies the object category — TABD for tables, DOMA for domains, DTEL for data elements, SHLP for search helps, ENQD for lock objects. ACTVT: 01=Create, 02=Change, 03=Display, 06=Delete, 07=Activate. A developer needs activities 01, 02, 07 to do full DDIC development. Activity 03 alone gives read-only SE11 access. DEVCLASS and OBJNAME can restrict to specific packages or object names for finer control. Basis team manages S_DEVELOP assignments — typically via role DEVELOPER role template.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Authorization setup for new project: Junior developers given S_DEVELOP with ACTVT 03 only — could view SE11 objects but not create. Senior developers given 01, 02, 07 with DEVCLASS = 'ZPROJECT*' — could create/change/activate only in project packages. Basis team had full S_DEVELOP. This prevented accidental changes to production-critical DDIC objects by junior team members.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: Developers ko S_DEVELOP with ACTVT '*' (all activities) aur OBJTYPE '*' aur DEVCLASS '*' dena — too wide. Restricting by DEVCLASS limits impact: developer can only modify objects in their assigned package. Also, ACTVT 16 (execute) is separate from create/change — always analyze needed activities carefully.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" S_DEVELOP role definition (PFCG):
" Object: S_DEVELOP
" DEVCLASS: ZPROJECT_SALES (specific package)
" OBJTYPE: TABD (tables only, or add DOMA, DTEL as needed)
" OBJNAME: * (all objects in that package)
" P_GROUP: * (all groups)
" ACTVT: 01, 02, 07 (create, change, activate)

" ABAP: check developer auth:
AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'S_DEVELOP'
  ID 'DEVCLASS' FIELD 'ZPROJECT_SALES'
  ID 'OBJTYPE'  FIELD 'TABD'
  ID 'OBJNAME'  FIELD '*'
  ID 'P_GROUP'  FIELD '*'
  ID 'ACTVT'    FIELD '07'.  " Can activate tables?
IF sy-subrc <> 0.
  MESSAGE 'Activation not authorized' TYPE 'E'.
ENDIF.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you use SU53 to diagnose a Data Dictionary authorization issue?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: SU53 user ke liye last failed authorization check display karta hai — authorization diagnosis mein sab se efficient first step. Procedure: user ko error trigger karne ko kaho, phir immediately SU53 open karo (ya user khud SU53 run kar sakta hai). SU53 dikhata hai: kaunsa authorization object failed, kaunse field names aur values check hue, aur user ke paas actually kya authorization hai vs kya needed tha. Ye information use karke PFCG pe jaao, user ka role dhundo, required field values ke saath missing authorization object add karo, save karo, profile generate karo, aur user ko assign karo. Production fixes ke liye, pehle development mein test karo, phir role change transport karo.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: SU53 displays the last failed authorization check for a user — the most efficient first step in authorization diagnosis. Procedure: ask the user to trigger the error, then immediately open SU53 (or the user can run SU53 themselves). SU53 shows: the authorization object that failed, the field names and values that were checked, and what authorization the user actually has versus what was needed. Use this information to go to PFCG, find the user's role, add the missing authorization object with the required field values, save, generate the profile, and assign to user. For production fixes, test in development first, then transport the role change.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: SU53 in action: User reported cannot view ZCFG_PLANT table in SE16. Called user, asked to try again, then ran SU53 in their session. SU53 showed: S_TABU_DIS, DICBERCLS = '&NC&', ACTVT = '03'. User had S_TABU_DIS with DICBERCLS = 'ZCFG' but table had auth group '&NC&'. Checked TDDAT: ZCFG_PLANT had auth group '&NC&' (no auth check — broad access). Fixed by adding '&NC&' to user's S_TABU_DIS, but then realized: '&NC&' should not exist in production tables. Changed table auth group to 'ZCFG' in TDDAT and TMG. User's existing role then worked correctly.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: Auth issue mein SU53 chalaye bina guess karna. Without SU53, developers often add wrong authorization objects to roles. SU53 gives exact answer in 30 seconds. Always use SU53 — no guessing needed.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" SU53 usage:
" After user sees authorization error:
" 1. User: type SU53 in transaction box → Enter
" 2. SU53 shows: Last Failed Authorization Check
" 3. Note: Object name, Field names, Required values

" Example SU53 output:
" Authorization Object: S_TABU_DIS
" Field DICBERCLS: Required 'ZCFG', User has 'ZTEST'
" Field ACTVT: Required '03', User has '02'

" ABAP: Simulate SU53 check programmatically:
AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'S_TABU_DIS'
  ID 'DICBERCLS' FIELD 'ZCFG'
  ID 'ACTVT'     FIELD '03'.
IF sy-subrc = 0.
  WRITE: / 'User is authorized'.
ELSE.
  WRITE: / 'Authorization failed - add ZCFG + 03 to role'.
ENDIF.

followupAnswerBank.question: What authorization is needed for SM30 table maintenance and how do you set it up?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: SM30 table maintenance ko table ke authorization group ke saath S_TABU_DIS authorization chahiye. Authorization group do places pe set hota hai: Table Maintenance Generator (SE11 mein Utilities → Table Maintenance Generator → Authorization Group field), aur TDDAT table mein stored. User ko full maintenance ke liye table ke authorization group ke saath matching DICBERCLS aur ACTVT 02 (change) ke saath S_TABU_DIS chahiye ya view only ke liye ACTVT 03 (display). Setup process: appropriate authorization group determine karo (config tables ke liye ZCFG jaisa meaningful name use karo), TMG mein set karo, ensure karo ki TDDAT mein entry hai (TMG ye automatically karta hai), PFCG mein appropriate roles mein us group ke saath S_TABU_DIS add karo, role changes ko production mein transport karo.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: SM30 table maintenance requires S_TABU_DIS authorization with the table's authorization group. The authorization group is set in two places: Table Maintenance Generator (Utilities → Table Maintenance Generator → Authorization Group field in SE11), and stored in TDDAT table. A user needs S_TABU_DIS with DICBERCLS matching the table's authorization group and ACTVT 02 (change) for full maintenance or ACTVT 03 (display) for view only. Setup process: determine appropriate authorization group (use a meaningful name like ZCFG for config tables), set it in TMG, ensure TDDAT has the entry (TMG does this automatically), add S_TABU_DIS with that group to appropriate roles in PFCG, transport role changes to production.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: SM30 setup for business users: Config tables ZCFG_ROUTES and ZCFG_PLANT needed maintenance by business configurators. Created authorization group 'ZCFG_CONF'. Both tables TMG regenerated with auth group 'ZCFG_CONF'. Created role Z_ROLE_CONFIG_MAINT with S_TABU_DIS DICBERCLS = 'ZCFG_CONF', ACTVT = '02, 03'. Assigned to 5 business configurators. Only those 5 users can maintain these tables via SM30 — other users see 'No authorization' error.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: SM30 ke liye authorization group '&NC&' production mein rakhna. '&NC&' = no authorization check = any logged-in user can maintain the table. Security risk. Always set a meaningful authorization group for config tables in production and restrict via roles.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Check what authorization group a table has:
SELECT tabname, cclass FROM tddat
  WHERE tabname = 'ZCFG_ROUTES'.
" If empty: no auth group set - anyone can view/change!

" Set auth group via SE11:
" SE11 → Table → Utilities → Table Maintenance Generator
" Authorization Group: ZCFG_CONF
" Generate/Overwrite

" PFCG role setup:
" PFCG → role Z_ROLE_CONFIG → Authorization tab
" Add: S_TABU_DIS
"   DICBERCLS: ZCFG_CONF
"   ACTVT:     02 (change), 03 (display)
" Generate profile → assign to users

followupAnswerBank.question: What is S_TABU_CLI and why is it sensitive in Data Dictionary context?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: S_TABU_CLI client-independent tables tak access control karta hai — wo tables jinme MANDT field nahi hai aur data system ke sab clients ke across share hota hai. Ek client-independent table change karna sab clients ko simultaneously affect karta hai — development, QA, aur agar table cross-system hai (jaise repository tables), potentially production data. Ye authorization sirf basis administrators aur system programmers ko diya jaana chahiye. DDIC mein common S_TABU_CLI scenarios: developers ko kabhi kabhi client-independent DDIC metadata tables (DD02L, DD03L, etc.) read karna padta hai — view access (ACTVT 03) acceptable ho sakti hai. Lekin change access (ACTVT 02) tightly controlled honi chahiye. Non-basis users ke liye change authorization ke saath S_TABU_CLI ka audit finding ek critical security finding hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: S_TABU_CLI controls access to client-independent tables — tables without MANDT field where data is shared across all clients in the system. Changing a client-independent table affects all clients simultaneously — development, QA, and if the table is cross-system (like repository tables), potentially production data. This authorization should only be given to basis administrators and system programmers. Common S_TABU_CLI scenarios in DDIC: developers sometimes need to read client-independent DDIC metadata tables (DD02L, DD03L, etc.) — view access (ACTVT 03) may be acceptable. But change access (ACTVT 02) must be tightly controlled. Audit finding of S_TABU_CLI with change authorization for non-basis users is a critical security finding.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Security audit finding: 12 developers had S_TABU_CLI with ACTVT 02 (change). Root cause: a template role had been copied and included S_TABU_CLI from the basis template. Developers theoretically could change client-independent tables via SE16N — including repository data. Risk: accidental or intentional corruption of system-wide configuration. Fix: S_TABU_CLI removed from all developer roles. If read access needed for specific client-independent tables, used S_TABU_DIS with those tables' specific auth groups instead.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: Role copy karte waqt S_TABU_CLI carry over hone dena without reviewing. Jab bhi role copy karo — sab authorization objects review karo. S_TABU_CLI with ACTVT 02 is always a red flag for non-basis users. Must be explicitly justified and approved.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Check if users have S_TABU_CLI:
" SUIM → Users by Authorization Values
" Object: S_TABU_CLI, ACTVT: 02
" This shows all users with client-independent change auth

" ABAP: Check authorization before client-indep table access:
AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'S_TABU_CLI'
  ID 'CLIIDMAINT' FIELD 'X'.
IF sy-subrc <> 0.
  MESSAGE 'No authorization for cross-client table' TYPE 'E'.
ENDIF.

" Find client-independent custom tables (no MANDT):
SELECT tabname FROM dd02l
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_ci)
  WHERE tabname LIKE 'Z%'
    AND as4local = 'A'
    AND NOT EXISTS (
      SELECT fieldname FROM dd03l
      WHERE tabname = dd02l~tabname AND fieldname = 'MANDT' ).

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you restrict access to specific fields in a custom table through authorization?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Data Dictionary khud field-level authorization ko directly implement nahi karta. Field-level access control program layer pe custom authorization objects use karke implement hota hai. Process: SU21 mein ek custom authorization object create karo (Object Class → Object → Fields — e.g., field ZACCESS aur values READ/WRITE/ADMIN ke saath object ZFLD_SALARY). Table display kar rahe ABAP program mein, sensitive fields display karne se pehle AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'ZFLD_SALARY' use karo. Result ke based pe, ya to field value display karo ya '***' ya blank dikhao. PFCG ke through authorization object values ko roles ko assign karo — finance managers WRITE lete hain, auditors READ lete hain, general users kuch nahi.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Data Dictionary itself does not implement field-level authorization directly. Field-level access control is implemented at the program layer using custom authorization objects. Process: create a custom authorization object in SU21 (Object Class → Object → Fields — e.g., object ZFLD_SALARY with field ZACCESS and values READ/WRITE/ADMIN). In the ABAP program displaying the table, use AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'ZFLD_SALARY' before displaying sensitive fields. Based on the result, either display the field value or show '***' or blank. Assign the authorization object values to roles via PFCG — finance managers get WRITE, auditors get READ, general users get nothing.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Requirement: Custom employee table had salary field ZSALARY. HR managers should see and edit salaries. Finance auditors should see but not edit. General users should not see salaries at all. Solution: created auth object ZOBJ_SALARY with field ZACCESS (values: DISP, EDIT). HR manager role: ZACCESS = EDIT. Auditor role: ZACCESS = DISP. Report: AUTHORITY-CHECK — if EDIT: show editable field; if DISP: show read-only; if neither: show '***'. Field-level security implemented cleanly without DDIC changes.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: SE11 mein field hide karna ya delete karna as a security measure. That breaks programs. Field-level security is always a program-layer concern — ABAP AUTHORITY-CHECK. SE11 is for structure definition, not security enforcement.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Custom auth object ZOBJ_SALARY (SU21):
" Object Class: ZHR
" Auth Object: ZOBJ_SALARY
" Field: ZACCESS (values: DISP, EDIT)

" ABAP program - field-level auth check:
DATA lv_salary_disp TYPE zcustom_emp-zsalary.

AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'ZOBJ_SALARY'
  ID 'ZACCESS' FIELD 'EDIT'.
IF sy-subrc = 0.
  lv_salary_disp = ls_employee-zsalary.  " Show actual salary
ELSE.
  AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'ZOBJ_SALARY'
    ID 'ZACCESS' FIELD 'DISP'.
  IF sy-subrc = 0.
    lv_salary_disp = ls_employee-zsalary.  " Read-only display
  ELSE.
    lv_salary_disp = '***RESTRICTED***'.  " No access
  ENDIF.
ENDIF.

followupAnswerBank.question: What authorization issues arise when transporting Data Dictionary objects?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: DDIC objects ke liye transport authorization S_TRANSPRT authorization object use karta hai. ACTVT 01 = transport request create karna, 02 = request contents change karna, 60 = transport ke liye request release karna, 70 = import. Developers typically ACTVT 01, 02, 60 rakhte hain — ye requests create, objects add, aur transport ke liye release kar sakte hain. Import (ACTVT 70) typically basis administrators aur controlled transport managers tak restricted hota hai. Production imports ke liye, additional authorization aur approval processes exist karte hain. Ek common authorization issue: developer transport release kar sakta hai lekin QA mein import nahi — QA system mein ACTVT 70 chahiye. Ye intentional hai — separation of duties: developer release karta hai, tester QA mein validate karta hai, basis production mein import karta hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Transport authorization for DDIC objects uses S_TRANSPRT authorization object. ACTVT 01 = create transport request, 02 = change request contents, 60 = release request for transport, 70 = import. Developers typically have ACTVT 01, 02, 60 — they can create requests, add objects, and release for transport. Import (ACTVT 70) is typically restricted to basis administrators and controlled transport managers. For production imports, additional authorization and approval processes exist. A common authorization issue: developer can release transport but cannot import to QA — requires ACTVT 70 in QA system. This is intentional — separation of duties: developer releases, tester validates in QA, basis imports to production.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Authorization design: Project had developers who could release transports (ACTVT 60) but not import to any system. QA system import was handled by a dedicated transport manager role (ACTVT 70 in QA). Production was strictly basis only. This segregation meant: developer finishes DDIC change, releases transport. Transport manager reviews and imports to QA. After QA testing sign-off, basis imports to production. Audit-compliant transport process with clear authorization boundaries.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: Developers ko production system mein S_TRANSPRT ACTVT 70 (import) dena. This violates segregation of duties — the developer who made the change should not also be able to deploy it to production unilaterally. Always separate: developer releases, independent basis/team imports to production.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Check transport authorization:
AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'S_TRANSPRT'
  ID 'TTYPE'  FIELD 'K'  " K = Workbench request
  ID 'ACTVT'  FIELD '60'. " Release authorization
IF sy-subrc <> 0.
  MESSAGE 'Cannot release transport - contact Basis' TYPE 'E'.
ENDIF.

" Find transports containing DDIC objects:
SELECT e070~trkorr, e071~object, e071~obj_name,
       e070~as4user, e070~trstatus
  FROM e070 JOIN e071 ON e070~trkorr = e071~trkorr
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_tr)
  WHERE e071~pgmid   = 'R3TR'
    AND e071~object   = 'TABD'  " TABD = table
    AND e070~trstatus = 'D'  " D = modifiable
  ORDER BY e070~as4date DESCENDING.

followupAnswerBank.question: How does the authorization group in Table Maintenance Generator impact security?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Table Maintenance Generator mein Authorization Group field SM30 table maintenance ke liye primary access control hai. Jab '&NC&' pe set hota hai, koi bhi user SM30 ke through table access kar sakta hai — development mein acceptable, production mein dangerous. Ek proper authorization group (configuration tables ke liye 'ZCFG' jaisa, finance tables ke liye 'ZFIN') SM30 access ko sirf un users tak restrict karta hai jinke roles mein us authorization group ke saath S_TABU_DIS included hai. Security implications: bina proper auth group ke SM30 ke through maintain hone wali financial data wali table = koi bhi business user accidentally ya maliciously critical financial configuration change kar sakta hai. Best practice: hamesha ek meaningful auth group set karo, us auth group ke saath ek dedicated role create karo, role sirf un users ko assign karo jinhe table maintain karne ki zaroorat hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: The Authorization Group field in Table Maintenance Generator is the primary access control for SM30 table maintenance. When set to '&NC&', any user can access the table via SM30 — acceptable in development, dangerous in production. A proper authorization group (like 'ZCFG' for configuration tables, 'ZFIN' for finance tables) restricts SM30 access to only users whose roles include S_TABU_DIS with that authorization group. Security implications: a table with financial data maintained via SM30 without proper auth group = any business user could accidentally or maliciously change critical financial configuration. Best practice: always set a meaningful auth group, create a dedicated role with that auth group, assign role only to users who need to maintain the table.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Security finding in production: 15 custom config tables all had authorization group '&NC&' — set by developers for convenience during development and never changed before production deployment. Any of the 500 business users could open SM30 and modify any of these config tables. Risk: accidental misconfiguration could break business processes. Fix: changed all tables to auth group 'ZCFG_PROD', created restricted maintainer role, assigned only to 8 designated configuration managers. Transport released to production. Security gap closed.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: Development mein '&NC&' set karo convenience ke liye — fine. Phir production transport ke waqt change karna bhool jaao. Add to transport checklist: verify authorization group of all custom tables before production transport. '&NC&' should never reach production for sensitive config tables.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Check all custom tables with &NC& auth group:
SELECT tabname, cclass
  FROM tddat
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_nc)
  WHERE tabname LIKE 'Z%'
    AND cclass = '&NC&'.
" These tables in production = security risk

" Change auth group (SE11 → Utilities → TMG → regenerate
" Or directly update TDDAT for quick fix):
MODIFY tddat SET cclass = 'ZCFG_PROD'
  WHERE tabname = 'ZCFG_CONFIG'
    AND cclass  = '&NC&'.
COMMIT WORK.
" Note: also need to regenerate TMG to match
" SE11 → Table → Utilities → TMG → Authorization Group: ZCFG_PROD
